title: How Do Today’s Young Adults Feel About Monogamy and Polyamory?
gist: A 2025 nationally representative survey of 22-35 year-olds finds monogamy remains the strong majority norm and desire, with only a small minority practicing polyamory, cutting against media narratives of collapsing sexual exclusivity.

insights.claim: Only 10% of young adults who expect to marry report being in a polyamorous relationship (7% of women, 13% of men), and it is unclear how committed these relationships are.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Actual polyamory practice is far smaller than cultural coverage implies — a signal to discount hype-driven bets on non-monogamy as a mainstream trend.

insights.claim: More than three-quarters (76%) of young adults who expect to marry agree that once you commit to a partner, intimacy with anyone else is never okay.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Traditional sexual ethics retain majority support in the marrying cohort despite a permissive media environment.
insights.quote: Once you commit to a partner, intimacy with anyone else is never okay
insights.locator: 3. Attitudes about monogamy still lean traditional

insights.claim: Polyamorous relationships remain rare among currently married couples, estimated at only 1-4%.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Baseline for tracking whether non-monogamy actually penetrates the institution of marriage over time.
insights.locator: Statistically, polyamorous relationships are still rare

insights.claim: Gender differences on monogamy attitudes are small, with young men only 4-8 percentage points more liberal than women and a solid majority of both endorsing monogamy.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Counters the narrative of a large emerging gender gap on relationship norms in the dating market.

insights.claim: About 1-in-5 (21%) young adults who expect to marry say they desire romantic relationships that do not require monogamy, at least during their dating years.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: A non-trivial minority is large enough to complicate dating dynamics even if it never reaches marriage.
insights.quote: About 1-in-5 (21%) young adults who expect to marry say that they desire romantic relationships that do not require monogamy.

insights.claim: Young adults who do not expect to marry are nearly twice as likely to be in a polyamorous relationship as those who do (18% vs 10%), and less likely to endorse exclusivity (61% vs 76%).
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Non-monogamy concentrates among the marriage-skeptical, so its prevalence tracks the broader retreat from marriage.
insights.locator: 5. Young adults who do not expect to marry

insights.claim: If even a modest preference for non-monogamy carries into marriage, it risks further deinstitutionalizing marriage by forcing spouses to negotiate exclusivity rather than assume it.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: The long-run risk to marriage is renegotiation of its core terms, not mass adoption of polyamory.
insights.locator: Moreover, if this modest level of preference

insights.claim: Scholarly treatment of consensual non-monogamy leans almost exclusively toward advocacy and seldom ventures into questioning, even as popular commentary is starting to voice doubts.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Flags an academic-consensus blind spot; the popular tide is turning more skeptical than the scholarship reflects.
insights.locator: Journalists and scholars continue to be intrigued

insights.claim: About 85% of single young adults still expect to marry one day, making the marriage-intending cohort the key group to monitor for shifts in relationship norms.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Marriage aspiration remains near-universal, underpinning demand for wedding, family and household formation over the long run.
insights.locator: Statistically, polyamorous relationships are still rare
